status: DRAFT — NOT PUBLISHED — SME REVIEW REQUIRED
disclaimer: EDUCATIONAL & PRE-TRAINING USE ONLY — NOT LEGAL ADVICE. These questions test the best-practice educational content of the course. They are not legal advice or Australian/New Zealand legal representation. Always verify against current legislation, the official Codes of Practice, Australian Standards and your state or territory WHS regulator before acting.
note: DRAFT end-of-course knowledge check for the CARSI-original 'Work Health and Safety (WHS) Fundamentals for Restoration and Cleaning Professionals' course. Structured identically to the other files in data/seed/assessment-drafts/ (quizId binds to the course's quiz lesson; questions/options/correctIndex/points/sourceRef). Every factual answer is anchored to a Tier-1 source recorded in docs/courses/whs-fundamentals-restoration/source-register.md. DO NOT seed or publish until the founder (SME) has reviewed each question and confirmed currency (see officialSourceRecheck in the course meta). cecHours is null — this course makes no IICRC CEC claim.

quizzes.title: WHS Fundamentals for Restoration and Cleaning — Knowledge Check

questions.questionText: How should you treat the guidance in this course?
options: As legally binding Australian legal advice you can rely on in court
As educational, best-practice material that you must verify against current law and your WHS regulator before acting
As a licence to perform asbestos removal and confined-space entry
As a replacement for the Safety Data Sheet

questions.questionText: In Australia, who ENFORCES work health and safety law (inspections, notices, prosecutions)?
options: Safe Work Australia
OSHA
Your state or territory WHS regulator (e.g. SafeWork NSW, WorkSafe Victoria)
The Fair Work Commission
questions.sourceRef: Module 1; Safe Work Australia is the policy body and does not enforce — https://www.safeworkaustralia.gov.au/

questions.questionText: Which Australian jurisdiction has NOT adopted the model WHS laws and runs its own Occupational Health and Safety Act 2004?
options: New South Wales
Queensland
Western Australia
Victoria

questions.questionText: When you apply the hierarchy of controls, where does personal protective equipment (PPE) sit?
options: First — put PPE on before anything else
Last — after elimination, substitution, isolation, engineering and administrative controls
It is not part of the hierarchy
Equal first with elimination
questions.sourceRef: Module 2; hierarchy of controls, PPE is the last line

questions.questionText: You are removing 12 m² of non-friable (bonded) asbestos cement sheet from a factory. What is required (outside the ACT)?
options: No licence — it is bonded, not friable
A Class B (or Class A) asbestos removal licence, because it is more than 10 m² of non-friable asbestos
Only a White Card
A Class A licence is the only option
questions.sourceRef: Module 3; WHS Regulation 487 — https://www.safeworkaustralia.gov.au/safety-topic/hazards/asbestos/whs-duties (ACT: any amount needs a licence)

questions.questionText: Since 1 July 2024, what is the status of engineered stone benchtops, panels and slabs in Australia?
options: Allowed if you wear a P2 respirator
The manufacture, supply, processing and installation is prohibited; import prohibited from 1 January 2025
Only banned in Victoria
Allowed for repairs but banned for new kitchens only

questions.questionText: You must cut and grind concrete and fibre-cement during a strip-out, and the work is assessed as high-risk crystalline silica processing. What must the PCBU do BEFORE work starts?
options: Nothing extra — just wear a dust mask
Prepare a written Silica Risk Control Plan, and arrange air monitoring, health monitoring and silica training
Notify OSHA
Only wet the surface
questions.sourceRef: Module 3; crystalline silica substances regime from 1 Sep 2024 — https://www.safeworkaustralia.gov.au/media-centre/news/stronger-regulation-crystalline-silica-substances-1-september-2024

questions.questionText: What is the Australian baseline respirator for mould and general fire/soot clean-up, and to which standard is it marked?
options: An N95, marked to NIOSH 42 CFR 84
A P2, marked to AS/NZS 1716 (fit-tested if tight-fitting)
A P1, marked to AS/NZS 1715
Any dust mask
questions.sourceRef: Module 4; AS/NZS 1716:2012 (device), AS/NZS 1715:2009 (selection/use); specify P2 not N95

questions.questionText: You arrive at a flooded home with standing water and running extraction gear. What is the correct electrical-safety approach?
options: Trust the home's own power points and safety switches
Ensure all portable equipment and leads are protected by a 30 mA RCD — bring your own portable RCD and confirm isolation before entering standing water near live circuits
Only use battery tools; mains is never safe
A GFCI on the US 120 V standard is fine
questions.sourceRef: Module 5; AS/NZS 3012 & AS/NZS 3760; 230 V/50 Hz; ~30 mA can be fatal

questions.questionText: A saturated subfloor with sewage needs entry. What must be in place before anyone goes in?
options: Just a P2 mask
A confined-space risk assessment, entry permit, atmospheric testing, ventilation, a standby person and a rescue plan (AS 2865 + Confined Spaces COP)
Nothing if it is only a small space
A White Card
questions.sourceRef: Module 5; AS 2865:2009 + Safe Work Australia Confined Spaces Code of Practice — no entry without a rescue plan

questions.questionText: Mould is present on a water-damaged job. What is the correct drying approach?
options: Run air movers immediately to dry fastest
Use air-filtration devices and dehumidifiers, and do NOT run air movers (which aerosolise spores) until the area is contained and cleared to Condition 1
Fog with antimicrobial and leave it
Open all windows and use pedestal fans
questions.sourceRef: Module 5; WHS duty + enHealth guidance + IICRC S520 as industry method; contain first, HEPA negative air

questions.questionText: A worker is taken to hospital as an in-patient after an incident on site. What are your duties?
options: Log it internally and continue work
Notify your state/territory WHS regulator immediately, keep records for at least 5 years, and preserve the incident site until an inspector or the regulator directs otherwise
Report to OSHA within 8 hours on a 300 log
Only notify if the worker does not return the next day
questions.sourceRef: Module 7; notifiable incidents (model WHS Act ss.35–39) + site preservation (s.39)
